coredata.dc:title: Sunlight promoted removal of toxic hexavalent chromium by cellulose derived photoactive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Aggarwal, Ruchi
Saini, Deepika
Sonkar, Sumit Kumar
Sonker, Amit Kumar
Westman, Gunnar
coredata.dc:description: A scalable synthetic procedure for fabricating photoactive carbon dots (CD) from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) is presented. The MCC was transformed into a photoactive nanosized CD by a one-step acid-assisted thermal-carbonization (~90 °C for 30 min). The efficiency of the obtained CD was determined by photo-removal of toxic hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) ions from wastewater. CD obtained from cellulose completely removed 20 ppm of Cr(VI) wastewater within ∼120 min under sunlight illumination. No Cr(VI) removal was observed in dark conditions and with control cellulose material as reference samples. The Cr(VI) removal follows pseudo-first-order kinetics along with a half-life of ∼26 min. Furthermore, the Cr(VI) removal from wastewater was supported via cyclic voltammetry analysis. Using a low-cost, naturally available cellulose material and sulfuric acid, the world's most-used chemical, creates techno-economic prerequisites for a scalable process of photoactive carbon dots.

(a) XPS survey scan of CD; the high-resolution survey scan of (b) C1s; (c) O1s and (d) S2p showing different binding energies.
Comparative analysis of cellulose and CD: (a) FT-IR; (b) Raman spectrum and (c) XRD.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A scalable synthetic procedure for fabricating photoactive carbon dots (CD) from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) is presented. The MCC was transformed into a photoactive nanosized CD by a one-step acid-assisted thermal-carbonization (~90 °C for 30 min). The efficiency of the obtained CD was determined by photo-removal of toxic hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) ions from wastewater. CD obtained from cellulose completely removed 20 ppm of Cr(VI) wastewater within ∼120 min under sunlight illumination. No Cr(VI) removal was observed in dark conditions and with control cellulose material as reference samples. The Cr(VI) removal follows pseudo-first-order kinetics along with a half-life of ∼26 min. Furthermore, the Cr(VI) removal from wastewater was supported via cyclic voltammetry analysis. Using a low-cost, naturally available cellulose material and sulfuric acid, the world's most-used chemical, creates techno-economic prerequisites for a scalable process of photoactive carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) (Sun et al., 2006) are explored and developed worldwide, mainly due to their attractive properties such as high specific surface area, low toxicity, excellent photoluminescence properties, biocompatibility (compared to heavy metal-based nanoparticles) (Han et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020a). With such properties, CD are envisaged to be part in the development of new materials in several areas such as biosensors (Dai et al., 2014), optoelectronic devices (Choi et al., 2013), solar cells (Dao et al., 2016), photocatalysis (Aggarwal et al., 2020), chemical sensing (Wu et al., 2018; Anand et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2021), electrocatalysis (Dong et al., 2014; Chellasamy et al., 2022), waste water treatment (Perumal et al., 2022) and bioimaging applications. (Zhu et al., 2013). CD can be synthesized using several different procedures such as; ablation (Gonçalves et al., 2010), hydrothermal (Anand et al., 2019; Shukla et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021), solvothermal treatment (Sun et al., 2021), ultrasonic treatment (Dang et al., 2016), microwave irradiation (Saini et al., 2020), electrochemical carbonization (Hou et al., 2015), electrochemical oxidation (Ming et al., 2012), etc. Another advantage is their ease of surface modification to induce passivation by introducing various surface-functionalities which may increase surface area and surface catalytic sites. (Yan et al., 2018; Yuan et al., 2018). However, apart from using the chemical precursors, CD and doped-CD can be fabricated from waste (Hernández-Cocoletzi et al., 2020), biomass (Chen et al., 2018; Chai et al., 2021), and biomass-derived waste that are inexpensive carbonaceous precursors, thus, significantly lowering overall cost and minimizing the use of any complicated experimental set-ups (Jonoobi et al., 2015; Meng et al., 2019).

Cellulose is a readily available natural resource with intrinsic renewable properties among the naturally occurring polymers. It is a natural polymeric material usually fractionated from coniferous and deciduous wood or cotton and isolated from various marine animals, fungi, bacteria, algae, amoeba, plants, etc (Siddhanta et al., 2013; Jonoobi et al., 2015). Depending on the cellulose source used, the hierarchical structure, especially the amount of amorphous and crystalline regions and cross section are different. Therefore, upon carbonization, the formed CD structure may show slightly different sizes and properties depending on which cellulose source has been used. Presently, cellulose has gained much attention in developing new materials (Hasanpour et al., 2021; Hong et al., 2021; Yap et al., 2021) as it is an economic biomass-material and environmentally friendly and does not compete with the food supply chain. At a low cost and as being a natural source, cellulose is a promising precursor for producing carbon-based nanomaterials.

CD has been used in various photocatalytic and electrocatalytic applications (Zhang et al., 2020b), but few reports are available on CD photocatalytic applications concerning Cr(VI) removal. Cr(VI) mainly occurs in areas where chromium-based leather tanning occurs and in electroplating, mining, and dyeing industries, leading to soil and water contamination (Aggarwal et al., 2019). Mainly, chromium shows two oxidation states, trivalent state (III) (a stable form) and hexavalent state (VI) (an oxidizing agent). Cr(III) is a vital trace element found in glucose and insulin metabolism, whereas Cr(VI) is toxic (Kan et al., 2017). Cr(VI) in the form of CrO4 2−or HCrO4 diffuse cell membranes and reach the bloodstream. The uptake damages the liver, kidney, and blood cells via different oxidation reactions (Jin et al., 2016). On a global scale, environmental clean-up and remediation processes for the proper management of brownfield lands demand Cr(VI) reduction or removal.

Various literature reports on Cr(VI) removal has been presented, but many of them are using expensive processes and require complex technical methods such as membrane nano-filtration (Ren et al., 2010; Yang et al., 2018), chemical precipitation (Xie et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018), ion-exchange (Alvarado et al., 2013), nanocomposite (Wang et al., 2018) etc. As a consequence, various reports on CD regarding Cr(VI) treatment as a practical and economical solution has been published using various approaches to improve the efficacy of the CD's (Choi et al., 2018; Bhati et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2020a).

Nevertheless, there are still demands for a simple, cost-effective, sustainable, and eco-friendly Cr(VI) removal method. Herein, we present a simple acid-assisted carbonization methodology for the fabrication of photoactive CD. The acidic hydrolysis pathway using sulfuric acid gives highly functional CD within 30 min at ~90 °C, which are considerable shorter reaction time and lower temperature than commonly used for CD preparation. After the carbonization and washing procedure, the black charred product was used to remove toxic Cr(VI) for wastewater treatment under sunlight illumination.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), Avicel ® PH 101, was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sulfuric Acid (H2SO4), Potassium Dichromate (K2Cr2O7), Diphenyl Carbazide (DPC), Absolute Acetone, para-Benzoquinone (p-BZQ), Potassium Persulphate (K2S2O8), Sodium salt of Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Na2-EDTA), tert-Butanol (t-BuOH) (99% assay) and tetra-n-Butyl Ammonium Bromide (TBAB) were of analytical grade and purchased from RANKEM. Absolute Ethanol for TEM. analysis was purchased from EMSURE.

Carbon dots (CD) was prepared by acid treatment of microcrystalline cellulose (MCC). The 200 mg of MCC was mixed with 2 mL of 98% pure Sulfuric acid in a beaker until the white powder was turned entirely into a black slurry material. The reaction was quenched by adding 10-fold DI water and then filtered using Whatman filter paper. Then the CD was washed with DI water until it became neutralized (pH ∼7). The black powder was dried in a hot air oven at ∼45 °C for an hour, and then it was ground with a motor-pestle to get homogenous CD particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalysis experiments for removal of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: For the Cr(VI) removal studies, a stock solution of (20 ppm) 20 mg potassium dichromate in 1L of deionized water was prepared and20 mL of Cr(VI) solution was transfered into a conical flask having 2.5 mg/mL of CD. The mixture was stirred for 5 min to reach the absorption-desorption equilibria. The solution was exposed to sunlight, and 500 μL aliquots was taken from the solution at an interval of 20 min, followed by centrifugation, for 3 min at 10,000 rpm, and then diluted 20 times. After this, the UV–visible absorption of the diluted solution was recorded at a fixed wavelength (540 nm) on the addition of a well-known DPC assay solution, which gives pink color with free Cr(VI) metal ions. Further, various parameters were analyzed towards the Cr(VI) removal efficiency using a 20 ppm stock solution. The photocatalysis experiment was done at MNIT Jaipur, India, in January 2021 and the global solar radiation was ∼536 W/m2 recorded according to weather database.

The photocatalytic removal of Cr(VI) ion using CD was examined using a three-electrode system at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1. The CV setup contained a working electrode (Pt electrode), an auxiliary electrode (Pt wire), and the reference electrode (Ag/AgCl). Also, 0.1 M TBAB solution was used as the supporting electrolyte. For electrochemical response, two samples, 20 ppm Cr(VI) solution (50 mL) as control and Cr(VI) treated wastewater were measured. Wastewater sample was solution of Cr(VI) (20 ppm, 50 mL) collected after its phototreatment with catalyst under sunlight illumination.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was characterized using the Tecnai 20 G2 300 kV STWIN model to analyze the morphology of CD. The sample for T.E.M. was prepared by diluting the sample in absolute Ethanol and then drop-casting it on a carbon-coated copper grid. An ESCA+ omicron nanotechnology oxford instrument was used for recording the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectra of CD. Titrations of CD was performed against NaOH to determine the sulfate content on CD surfaces using Titrando instrument from Metrohm. The Confocal Raman Spectrometer with P.L. with a 532 nm laser was used for Raman spectra of CD in the range of 100–2700 cm−1. The UV–Visible spectra was recorded in the wavelength range of 400–650 nm using Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer. The functional groups were examined by FT-IR spectrophotometer (a Bruker Vertex 70 model) with the spectra lying in the wavenumber range 4000-500 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured using Proto AXRD Benchtop Powder Diffractometer HS Code-90221990 with the spectra in the range of 10–80° 2θ angle. The setup PG-Lyte 1.2 model from Kanopy Techno Solutions Pvt. Ltd. Was used for performing Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments.

The synthesis of photoactive CD from MCC (microcrystalline cellulose) is presented in Scheme 1 . Cellulose was carbonized in concentrated sulfuric acid at ~90 °C for 30 min. During carbonization, cellulose follows a complex chemical reaction pathway likely by a simultaneous combination of polymerization (condensation and addition), dehydration and aromatization, formation of the carbon material with sulfonic groups, and other gaseous products such as H2O, CO, and CO2 (Carlson et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2009; Johar et al., 2012). The obtained black color slurry was further neutralized by water to get a cellulose-derived photoactive CD as a black powder material. The as-synthesized CD was investigated for the removal of Cr(VI) under the presence of sunlight, DPC assay was used to monitor the decrement in the concentrations of the Cr(VI).

The morphology of as-synthesized CD was analyzed by TEM . Fig. 1 (a) shows agglomerated quasi-spherical morphology of CD (da Silva Souza et al., 2018). The average size diameter distribution of CD corresponding to Fig. 1(a) lies in the range of ∼10–14 nm, as depicted by the histogram in Fig. 1(b), which is a unique structure. Since, normally cellulose yields nanosized crystals (5–10 nm wide and 20–30 nm length) (Dong et al., 1998) upon acid hydrolysis. However, in this study, cellulose is entirely carbonized using sulfuric acid and gives carbon dots (Wang et al., 2007). Mosaic patterns in lattice fringes were observed for CD, as shown in Fig. 1(c), and the corresponding zoomed image shown in Fig. 1(d) reveals an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to (100) lattice plane of graphite (Liao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020b).

The elemental composition and the different states of chemical groups present on the surface of CD was investigated by XPS analysis. The full survey scan XPS spectra show three significant peaks at ∼283.5 eV, ∼531.6 eV, and ∼167.2 eV corresponding to C1s, O1s, and S2p Fig. 2 (a) respectively. The percentage elemental composition of C1s, O1s, and S2p was observed to be ∼70.5, ∼25.7, and ∼3.8%, respectively, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 (a). The sulfur content was also determined by titration (Dong et al., 1998) (supporting information) and gave slightly higher value, (3.9% ± 0.3), than the obtained values from the XPS analysis. The results reveal that besides the abundant amount of carbon and oxygen on the surface of the obtained CD, mainly coming from cellulose, it also contains some sulfur due to sulfonation. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows the signals of four distinct carbon states –CC, C-C, –C–O/S, and –CO present at ∼ 284.2, ∼285.3, ∼286.2, and ∼288.4 eV, respectively (Baik et al., 2017; Kaushik et al., 2021b). The high-resolution O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2(c) has two peaks at ∼531.3 and ∼532.7 eV, attributed to –O–C/S groups and (-O=C-OH), respectively (Saini et al., 2020). The high-resolution S2p spectrum shows three peaks at ∼167.09, ∼168.29 and 139.64 eV in (Fig. 2(d)), which indicates the existence of Sulfur in the form of –C-SOx, where x = 2, 3, and 4 respectively. (Sun et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2015) (Wang et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier-Transformation Infrared (FT-IR), Raman and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The structural analysis of CD (black color) after carbonization of cellulose (blue color) was performed by comparative analysis of FT-IR, Raman, and XRD, as displayed in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR spectrum of cellulose in Fig. 3(a) shows typical absorption at ∼3390 cm−1 attributed to the –OH stretching vibration. The bands at ∼2900 cm−1 and ∼1442 cm−1 are attributed to –CH stretching and –CH2 bending mode. The band at ∼1374 cm−1 is due to the asymmetric stretching mode of the –C–O–C bond. The pyranose ring skeleton vibration of saccharide structure is observed at ∼1164, ∼1112, ∼1058, and ∼1032 cm−1, showing typical glycosidic linkages. Furthermore, the band at ∼896 cm−1 is reasonably due to glycosidic linkage between sugar units(Shen et al., 2016; Lv et al., 2017; da Silva Souza et al., 2018; Trilokesh and Uppuluri, 2019). After carbonization, surface groups formed on CD are identified in Fig. 3(a). The broad band, centered at ∼3240 cm−1 is due to –OH stretching. During the carbonization of cellulose destruction of the saccharide structure occur. This can be seen in changes of –C–O of different functional groups. The peak at ∼1717 cm−1 is due to –CO and ∼1210 cm−1 due to –C–O, attributed mainly to ethers, carboxylic acids, and lactones (da Silva Souza et al., 2018). The band at ∼1608 cm−1 is characteristic for –CC from an aromatic framework of CD. Also, –CH deformation of aromatics is observed from a small peak at ∼1366 cm−1 that appeared due to –CH3 bending vibration and peak at ∼805 cm−1 is due to –CH bending vibrations (da Silva Souza et al., 2016). The peak at ∼1035 cm−1 signifies the presence of –SO bond in the CD framework, which might be due to sulfonation of cellulose (Huang and Zhang, 2010; Cailotto et al., 2018).

The comparative Raman spectra in Fig. 3(b) clarify the structural changes between cellulose and CD. The cellulose shows an angular deformation present in the cellulosic structure in the region of ∼260–550 cm−1 as A0 and B0 bands. The axial deformation due to –C–O in cellulose is located in the region of ∼845–1210 cm−1 as B0 and A0 band, whereas the angular deformation modes are observed in the hydroxyl and methylene groups in the region ∼1210-1505 cm−1 corresponds to A0 band (Wiley and Atalla, 1987; Szymańska-Chargot et al., 2011; da Silva Souza et al., 2018). Raman spectra of the CD confirmed graphitization and surface defects formed after the carbonization of cellulose. Two intense peaks, one at ∼1580.4 cm−1 (G band), correspond to well-organized and in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms and ∼1371.9 cm−1 (D band), indicate the presence of surface defects on CD. The ratio (ID/IG) of integrated areas of both the peak is measured as 1.38, which refers to the high surface defects/more oxygenated species in CD after carbonization of cellulose material (Jayaweera et al., 2019).

Additionally, the XRD (Fig. 3(c)) was performed to verify the changes in cellulose structure after carbonization. The diffraction patterns of cellulose are observed at 2θ = ∼15.56°, ∼22.75°, and ∼34.67°, which are characteristic peaks corresponding/analogous to the lattice planes (110), (200) and (004) (Plermjai et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). The diffraction patterns of CD are observed at two 2θ values of ∼27.1° and ∼39.1° and indicated the changes by converting cellulose to graphitic nanostructures. The first peak at ∼27.1° is in agreement with the (002) planes of graphitic carbon (d002 = 3.35), and the other peak at ∼39.1° typically corresponds to the graphitic interplanar stacking (100) (Lv et al., 2017; Liao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic removal of toxic Cr(VI) ions using CD
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic experiment was performed for the remediation of Cr(VI) wastewater at different conditions and determined by using a UV–Visible spectrometer. Initially, the photocatalyst activity was optimized under light conditions (sunlight as a natural, freely available source of light and energy) and complemented by parallel control experiments. For this, a loading amount of 2.5 mg/mL of catalyst was added into a 20 mL solution of 20 ppm Cr(VI) ions and stirred in dark conditions for ∼5 min to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium between Cr(VI) and CD was attained. After this, the solution was exposed to dark conditions and under sunlight with another solution as control (where no catalyst (CD) was present) and control commercially available cellulose. 500 μL of the aliquot sample was withdrawn at regular intervals and centrifuged to separate catalyst from aqueous media. This centrifuged solution was treated with DPC assay (Khare et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018) and the absorption at 540 nm wavelength was recorded to monitor the decrement in Cr(VI) concentrations. The observation revealed that the control Cr(VI) (without catalyst), control pristine cellulose, and Cr(VI) with catalyst under dark conditions had no significant effect (∼5%) on the Cr(VI) removal as can be seen in Fig. 4 (a). At the same time, the Cr(VI) solution with catalyst and sunlight irradiation gave efficient removal result (∼99%) Fig. 4(a). Consequently, sunlight is required for CD to act as catalyst for Cr(VI) ions. Further optimization studies were done using different doses of photocatalyst from 0 to 3.5 mg/mL for the removal of 20 ppm Cr(VI) with sunlight as a continuous source. Fig. 4(b) shows the dose-dependency results for removal of 20 ppm Cr(VI). From the plot, C/C0 versus time graph, it is shown, as the dose of catalyst increase, a higher amount of Cr(VI) ions get removed from the system. This is most probably due to more number of active sites available for the reaction. For remaining experiments, a fixed dose of 2.5 mg/mL photocatalyst was used, and varied the concentrations of Cr(VI) ions (from 20 to 100 ppm). The result obtained from this experiment is displayed in Fig. 4(c), where ∼20 ppm was completely removed within the time frame of ∼120 min under the presence of sunlight. The concentration-dependent experiment specifies that on increasing Cr(VI) concentration, the number of metal ions also increases, which creates hindrance of photons to reach photocatalyst. Hence, fewer sites are available for the removal process, resulting in a decrement in the removal rate. The removal of Cr(VI) ions was further studied by varying Cr(VI) concentration and plotting the data in terms of ln(C0/C) versus time which fits into the pseudo first-order kinetic model with regression relative coefficient value (R2 > 0.80) as demonstrates from Fig. 4(d) and hence follows the Langmuir−Hinshelwood (L-H) kinetic model. The Cr(VI) removal follows pseudo-first-order kinetics as per the following equation.
ln
(
C o
C
)
= k t

C0 and C is the concentration of Cr(VI) at 0 min and at time t, respectively, and k refers to the rate constant for the pseudo-first-order kinetics. Fig. 4(e) is the corresponding rate constant (∼0.026 min−1) and half-life time (∼26 min), respectively. Along with this optimization, a recycling study was also done, following the same experimental procedure. The catalyst shows good recyclability up to ∼4 cycles. For the recycle study, the solid residue was collected, which remains after Cr(VI) removal, and centrifugated, followed by thorough washing with distilled water (as Cr(VI) is highly water-soluble and easily eliminated), followed by drying at ∼45 °C and then reused. The change in activity of the reused photocatalyst for metal ion removal, are shown in Fig. 4(f).

Although, the reusability of obtained CD is limited, it should be possible to increase up to ∼20 cycles. This can be achieved by further doping with metal and non-metal ions such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorous, zinc oxide, copper, cadmium sulfite, etc. which will alter the band gap of catalyst and increase its efficiency and can be explored in other fields too.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism pathway for Cr(VI) removal
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization studies show that CD successfully remove Cr(VI) ions only under sunlight conditions. Thus, the removal process is only successful when CD can produce other active species. To gain insight in what type of active specie(s) are formed, quenching experiment for trapping of those active groups was done. Four different types of scavengers (Aggarwal et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2021a) were used; tert-butanol (t-BuOH to trap hydroxyl radicals (•OH), para-benzoquinone (p-BZQ to trap superoxide radicals (O2 •−), potassium persulphate (K2S2O8 for trapping electrons (e−) and sodium salt of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Na2EDTA to trap holes (h+).(Saini et al., 2019) We performed the same experiment condition using 50 mM of scavengers in the 20 ppm Cr(VI) solution, 20 mL with another set as control where no scavenger was present. It's clearly seen in Fig. 5 (a) that e− are mainly responsible for the removal process of Cr(VI) as scavenger K2S2O8 traps all the e− and the removal process are inhibited (∼60% removal only) .

On the other hand, the other scavengers show a comparatively lesser pronounced effect in decreasing the removal efficiency towards Cr(VI) ions (>70% removal done). Considering the above observation, the pictorial representation of a plausible mechanism for removing Cr(VI) ion in the presence of CD is shown in Fig. 5(b). The step-wise and detailed description of the plausible mechanism followed by CD for removing Cr(VI) is shown in the figure in 5(c). Initially, upon the irradiation of sunlight, the photoexcitation of e− from the valence band (V.B.) of CD to the conduction band (C.B.) leads to photoinduced charge carriers (Barrera-Díaz et al., 2012). Sometimes, retardation in the electron-hole pair recombination also occurs due to trapping photo-excited e− by the structural or surface defects (Zhao et al., 2021) and forms highly reactive protons (H+) ions from water molecules. The combination of H+ ions and e−in the solution reduces the toxic Cr(VI) ions into lower oxidation states (preferably to the state of Cr(III)). Additionally, the sunlight assisted removal of Cr(VI) ions to less toxic oxidation state was shown by a decrease in intensity of pink color of the Cr(VI)-complex in DPC assay test, shown in Fig. 5 (d).

Cyclic voltammetry was used to further support CD photoactivity towards removing toxic Cr(VI) metal ions (Fig. 6 ). In an aqueous solution, control Cr(VI) (blue line) displayed quasi-reversible redox response at E 1/2 = −0.512 V vs Ag/AgCl (E
oxi = −0.493 V with −0.0021 mA peak current vs. Ag/AgCl and E
red = −0.531 V with 0.0816 mA peak current vs. Ag/AgCl). Redox responses for Cr(VI) in wastewater (black line), in presence of CD under sunlight were appeared at E
oxi = −0.309 V with −0.00134 mA peak current vs. Ag/AgCl and E
red = −0.051 V with −0.0674 mA peak current vs. Ag/AgCl. On considering redox responses from both the samples, Cr(VI) treated wastewater with CD show anodic shift due to the irreversible electrochemical reduction of Cr(VI) ions to its lower oxidation state, preferably to Cr(III) ions in comparison to the control sample. (Wyantuti et al., 2015; Prabhakaran et al., 2017; JSS, 2020). It indicates that CD promotes the reduction of Cr(VI) to lower oxidation states.

Cellulose is a low-cost, green, and universally available biomass that forms photoactive CD under simple acid-assisted carbonization. The successful carbonization of cellulose is performed at ~90 °C in 30 min which is much lower and shorter than normal carbonization conditions (Temperature >250 °C, Time > 2hrs). The obtained CD was reusable and removed hexavalent chromium Cr(VI) from wastewater by sunlight irradiation. The removal of Cr(VI) was monitored by UV–visible spectrometer and supported by cyclic voltammogram. The removal mechanism was found to be sequential adsorption to the CD and then removing Cr(VI) due to the formation of electrons in the presence of sunlight. The ease of availability of cellulose material as a natural carbon precursor to produce CD makes the overall process economical and sustainable. Also, the prepared CD can be modified and applied in sensing, imaging, and biological activity in the future.

G. W., A. K. S. and S. K. S. planned and supervised all experiments of the work; R. A and D. S. executed most of the experiment work and performed all the experiments. All authors reviewed the manuscript.
